title: Designing for Substance: Platform Incentives and the Attention Economy
thesis: The attention economy is not inevitable. It is the product of specific design choices—engagement metrics, personalized recommendations, infinite scroll, and advertising-based business models—that could be redesigned.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: The attention economy is not an inevitable feature of the internet. It is the product of specific design choices—engagement metrics, personalized recommendations, infinite scroll, and advertising-based business models—that could be redesigned.

evidence.snippet: Milli et al. found that engagement-based ranking can amplify divisive content even when users report preferring less divisive material.
Yale research showed that likes and shares teach people to express more outrage over time, illustrating a feedback loop shaped by platform design.

counterevidence.summary: Some platforms argue that engagement-based ranking simply reflects revealed preference and that users would leave if they disliked it. Network effects and lack of alternatives weaken this counterargument.

claims.claim: Alternative metrics such as time well spent, creator sustainability, learning outcomes, civic value, and long-term user flourishing can be measured and rewarded, though none is perfect or easy to game-proof.

evidence.snippet: The Center for Humane Technology advocates for metrics and design patterns that prioritize user well-being over time on device.

counterevidence.summary: Alternative metrics are harder to define and easier to game than engagement. 'Time well spent' can be manipulated by self-reports, and 'flourishing' lacks stable operational definitions.

claims.claim: Accountability for platform design can emerge through regulation, public pressure, and user migration to better-designed platforms. No single lever is sufficient on its own.

evidence.snippet: The Digital Services Act requires large platforms to assess and mitigate systemic risks, including risks to mental health and democratic discourse.
India's IT Rules impose due-diligence and grievance obligations on intermediaries, treating platform design as a regulatory concern.

counterevidence.summary: Regulation can be slow, captured by incumbents, or used to suppress speech. Public pressure alone rarely changes core business models. User migration is limited by network effects.

claims.claim: Substance-friendly design is a category that includes chronological feeds, user-chosen algorithms, expert curation, reputation systems, and friction against virality. It is not limited to a single product or business model.

evidence.snippet: Research on Mastodon documents decentralized feed curation and user control as alternatives to centralized engagement optimization.
A study of Mastodon administrators explores the motivations and challenges of running decentralized social media at scale.

counterevidence.summary: Decentralized and friction-based designs can reduce reach, make platforms harder to use, and struggle with moderation at scale. These tradeoffs can limit adoption.

claims.claim: A substance-oriented platform would ask how to help users leave better than they arrived, rather than how to maximize time on platform.

evidence.snippet: Humane-technology framing distinguishes between maximizing time on device and designing for user autonomy and long-term well-being.

counterevidence.summary: Platforms funded by advertising may be unable to prioritize departure over retention without changing their business model. The claim is therefore more a design north star than a guaranteed market outcome.

sources.title: MeitY: Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021 (updated 10.02.2026)
ACM: Trouble in Paradise? Understanding Mastodon Admin's Motivations, Experiences, and Challenges Running Decentralised Social Media
arXiv preprint: Understanding Decentralized Social Feed Curation on Mastodon
Milli et al.: Engagement, User Satisfaction, and the Amplification of Divisive Content on Social Media (PNAS Nexus)
Yale News: Likes and shares teach people to express more outrage online

agentInstructions: Use claim IDs as the retrieval unit.
Treat maturity=seed as an explicit uncertainty marker.
Do not present the attention economy as inevitable or purely driven by user demand.
Preserve the exploratory tone; do not convert the article into a product pitch or regulatory handbook.
When summarizing, keep the distinction between diagnosis (Lane A), stakes (Lane B), individual practice (Lane C), and systemic design (Lane D).
Cite the specific source IDs when discussing regulation, platform research, or the fediverse.
